coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared light photocatalytic ability for degradation of tetracycline using carbon dots modified Ag/AgBr nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weilong
Lv, Huachang
Yuan, Songliu
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Ever-increasing environmental concerns have stimulated great progresses in photocatalytic technology in collection with the environmental remediation and solar energy utilization. In this study, carbon dots (CDs)/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites were prepared via a facile precipitation-photoreduction method. Moreover, CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites show a strong absorbance in the full solar spectrum due to the interaction of Ag nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) and CDs. Meanwhile, compared with pristine AgBr and Ag/AgBr nanoparticles, a dramatic enhancement in the degradation of tetracycline under near-infrared light irradiation is observed over the CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites. Significantly, the extraordinary photocatalytic performance is attributed to the extended absorption in the full spectrum, effective charge separation, and synergistic enhancement induced by surface plasmon resonance of Ag-NPs and the photogenerated change transfer properties of CDs in the complex nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Ag/AgBr
Near-infrared
Nanocomposites
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology , the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) ( 2012CB825803 and 2013CB932702 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 11174092 , 11474111 , 51422207 , 51132006 , 51572179 , 21471106 , and 21501126 ), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education ( 20123201110018 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China ( BK20140310 , BK20141305 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2014M560445 , 2015T80581 ), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of AgBr (a), Ag/AgBr (b) and CDs/Ag/AgBr (c).
SEM image (a), TEM image (b) and HRTEM (c and d) images of CDs/Ag/AgBr.
Raman (a) and FT-IR (b) spectra of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr.
The full XPS spectrum (a) of CDs/Ag/AgBr and the high-resolution XPS spectra (b–d) of C1s, Ag 3d (c) and Br 3d (d) in CDs/Ag/AgBr, respectively.
UV–vis–NIR diffusive reflectance spectra of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr. Inset: digital photograph of AgBr (a), Ag/AgBr (b) and CDs/Ag/AgBr (c).

Photocatalytic degradation of TC with AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr as photocatalysts under visible light (λ>420nm) (a) and (b) under near-infrared light (λ>750nm). Photocatalytic degradation rate of TC with CDs/Ag/AgBr sample in the NIR light for 30min (λ>750nm) and heat at 70°C in the dark for 30min (c), the repeated degradation of TC over recycled CDs/Ag/AgBr photocatalysts under near-infrared light (d).
Transient photocurrent responses (λ>750nm) (a) and EIS Nyquist plots (b) of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr.

Photoluminescence spectra of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr (a). Effects of a series of scavengers on the TC degradation over CDs/Ag/AgBr under near-infrared light (b). The ESR spectra of CDs/Ag/AgBr under dark or near-infrared light (λ >750nm): DMPO-
O2 − in methanol dispersions (c); DMPO-
OH in aqueous dispersions (d).
The reaction mechanism for degradation of TC over CDs/Ag/AgBr under near-infrared light.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared light photocatalytic ability for degradation of tetracycline using carbon dots modified Ag/AgBr nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Suzhou, 215123 Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu United Chemical Co., Ltd., East of Yangzhong Bridge, 212212 Yangzhong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ever-increasing environmental concerns have stimulated great progresses in photocatalytic technology in collection with the environmental remediation and solar energy utilization. In this study, carbon dots (CDs)/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites were prepared via a facile precipitation-photoreduction method. Moreover, CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites show a strong absorbance in the full solar spectrum due to the interaction of Ag nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) and CDs. Meanwhile, compared with pristine AgBr and Ag/AgBr nanoparticles, a dramatic enhancement in the degradation of tetracycline under near-infrared light irradiation is observed over the CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites. Significantly, the extraordinary photocatalytic performance is attributed to the extended absorption in the full spectrum, effective charge separation, and synergistic enhancement induced by surface plasmon resonance of Ag-NPs and the photogenerated change transfer properties of CDs in the complex nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis, as a new developing discipline, has been one of the hottest spots in the field of science and technology, which plays a significant role in the solutions of energy development and environmental pollution [1–3]. On account of solar energy utilization, to develop novel and efficient semiconductor photocatalysts that can harvest full solar spectrum, from ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR), is still one of the most pressing tasks for current photocatalytic system [4]. Nevertheless, a great deal of research has focused on the wide band gap semiconductors (such as TiO2, ZnO, and ZnS) during the past few decades, which can be merely excited under UV light accounting for only 5% of the solar spectrum [5–9]. In order to overcome this shortcoming, strenuous studies have been focused on designing photocatalysts that can be excited in the range of visible (Vis) light, which accounts for about 48% of the incoming solar energy [10]. Regrettably, NIR light which accounts for 44% of the solar spectrum, remains unutilized, which has become a challenging mission for chemistry and materials science [11].

Recently, carbon-based materials, such as carbon nanofibers, carbon nanotubes, graphene and carbon nanodots, have been widely introduced due to their excellent conductivity and extraordinary chemical stability [12–15]. Among various nanostructured carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) with excellent photo-physical and chemical properties are receiving increasing attention [16]. In view of the outstanding photo-generated electron transfer properties of CDs, many CDs-based complex photocatalysts (CDs/TiO2, CDs/SiO2 and CDs/Ag3PO4) have been designed to display efficient photocatalytic activities under visible light irradiation [17–19], while their photocatalytic efficiencies were still poor under the NIR light illumination. More recently, the photocatalytic properties of Ag/AgBr have been diffusely developed [20–22]. Due to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Ag nanoparticles (Ag-NPs), Ag/AgBr nanoparticles exhibit strong absorption in the visible region, enhance photo-induced electrons transfer and thus improve photocatalytic performance in decomposing organic pollutants. Based on its relatively narrow band gap (around 2.6eV), high photocatalytic activity and photostability [23,24], Ag/AgBr is a potential candidate to design the CDs-based NIR light driven photocatalyst.

Herein, we firstly prepared CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites via a facile precipitation-photoreduction method. Remarkably, the CDs/Ag/AgBr exhibits outstanding NIR photocatalytic ability for degradating tetracycline (TC) compared to that of AgBr and Ag/AgBr as catalysts. Significantly, the extraordinary photocatalytic performance is attributed to the extended absorption in the full spectrum, effective charge separation, and synergistic enhancement induced by the SPR of Ag-NPs and the photogenerated change transfer properties of CDs in the complex nanocomposites.
All chemical reagents of analytical grade were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China, and used without further purification.

CDs were prepared via an electro-chemical method in our previous report [25]. The typical steps are noted as follow. Firstly, two graphite rods were placed into 1000mL ultrapure water. Afterwards, 30V potential was applied to the graphite rods. After constant stirring for 120h, a black solution was obtained in the reactor. Lastly, the CDs solution was filtered and obtained by filter paper. The dark CDs solution was then dried and weighted, confirming the CDs solution concentration of 500mg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr
ce:section.ce:para: 0.119g potassium bromide (KBr) and 0.5g polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were uniformly dissolved in 30mL deionized water. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) solution (0.04M) was added into the above solution, stirring for 4h in the dark under ordinary temperature. The obtained AgBr powder was washed with distilled water three times, and dried at 70°C for 10h. As for Ag/AgBr, the resulting suspension containing components of KBr, PVP and AgNO3 was oddly needed to irradiate under a Xe lamp with 300mWcm−2 illumination intensity for another 4h. The CDs/Ag/AgBr photocatalyst was produced by displacing the CDs solution with water. A pre-determined volume of CDs solution (corresponding to 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50mL) was used for the synthesis. Preliminary experiments indicated that the composite synthesized by addition of 30mL CDs solution displayed the best photocatalytic activity under NIR light irradiation (Fig. S1). Thus, this condition was selected to synthesize the ternary composite and the product was labeled as CDs/Ag/AgBr.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of samples were recorded on an X’Pert-ProMPD (Holand) D/max-γAX-ray diffractometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray analysis spectroscopy (EDX) were characterized by an FEI-quanta 200 scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken on an FEI/Philips Tecnai 12 BioTWIN transmission electron microscope and a CM200 FEG transmission electron microscope, respectively. Raman spectra were obtained on an HR 800 Raman spectroscope The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of samples was collected on a Nicolet-360 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed on an ESCALABMK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. UV–vis–NIR absorption was recorded on a Lambda 750 (Perkin-Elmer) spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) study was recorded by a Fluorolog-TCSPC Luminescence Spectrometer.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted on a CHI 660B workstation with a standard three-electrode cell. The Pt wire, a calomel electrode, and the samples were used as the counter electrode, the reference electrode, and the working electrode, respectively. The synthesis of working electrodes was in line with the previous literature [26]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded by applying an AC voltage of 5mV amplitude in the frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz with the open circuit voltage in 0.5M Na2SO4. For photocurrent time (PT) measurements, a 300W Xe lamp (cutting off λ
< 750nm) served as near-infrared source, and a Na2SO4 (0.5M) aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte.

In a typical photocatalytic activity test, 100mg photocatalyst was suspended in 100mL aqueous solution of 10mg/L tetracycline (TC). Prior to visible light illumination, the solution was stirred for 30min without light to ensure the establishment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Vis or NIR light was obtained by using a 300W xenon lamp and cutting off filters to remove light of λ
< 420 or 750nm. During the irradiation, 3mL aliquots were removed at certain time intervals and analysed on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi UV-3100) to record the changes of concentration. Before the spectroscopy measurement, these photocatalysts were removed from the photocatalytic reaction systems by centrifugation.

The electron spin resonance (ESR) technique was conducted with an electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (A300-10/12, Bruker) to further used to detect the presence of
OH and
O2 − radicals in the photocatalytic reaction system under near-infrared light irradiation (λ
> 750nm). Before to determine the hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals, 10.0mg samples were dissolved in 0.5mL deionized water (DMPO-
OH) or 0.5mL methanol (DMPO-
O2 −), and then 45μL DMPO was added with ultrasonic dispersion for 5min, respectively.

The preparation of CDs from graphite rods could be achieved via an electro-chemical method and the TEM images and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs were shown in Fig. S2, which are in good agreement with CDs structure publicly reported [27]. The phases of the as-prepared photocatalysts were identified by XRD characterization. As shown in Fig.1 a, all the characteristic diffraction peaks of pure AgBr are perfectly in agreement with the JCPDS card No. 06-0438 [28]. For Ag/AgBr in Fig.1b, besides the peaks of AgBr, the diffraction peak at 2θ=38.2° can be indexed to metallic Ag0 (JCPDS 04-0783) [29]. Particularly, the XRD pattern of CDs/Ag/AgBr (Fig.1c) is similar to the Ag/AgBr (Fig.1b) and the characteristic peak for carbon at 26° is not found, which may be assigned to the slight quantity of CDs in the nanocomposites [30].

The structure and morphology of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig.2 a, the SEM of CDs/Ag/AgBr suggests that Ag-NPs with diameter of 5–20nm are formed on the surfaces of AgBr particles with diameter of 40–100nm. However, CDs are difficult to discern through SEM because of their very small size. In order to ensure the existence of carbon element, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) element mapping images of CDs/Ag/AgBr were recorded. As shown in Fig. S3, the elements of C, Ag and Br can be achieved. In addition, the TEM image in Fig.2b further reveals the complex structure of the CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites. Furthermore, the HRTEM images (Fig.2c and d) of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites show that there are three kinds of lattice fringes with the different lattice spacings, and the insoluble CDs layer is on the surface of AgBr. The fringe spacing of 0.285nm is ascribed to the (200) lattice plane of AgBr [31], and the lattice fringe spacing of 0.234nm is agreed well with the (111) plane of Ag-NPs [32]. The fringe with the interplanar spacing of 0.212nm is attributed to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon [33], which is a strong supporting evidence of the formation of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites. Additionally, the EDS analysis of as-prepared products also confirms the formation of the CDs/Ag/AgBr (Fig. S4).

Raman spectra of as-prepared nanocomposites are shown in Fig.3 a. It is found that there are no obvious peaks originated from AgBr and Ag/AgBr samples. Contrarily, two characteristic peaks located at about 1335 and 1607cm−1 are observed in the Raman spectra of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites, corresponding to the D-band and G-band of carbon, respectively. The D-band is disorder-induced because of the defects, dislocations and lattice distortions in carbon structures [34,35]. The G-band can be interpreted as the stretching vibration mode of graphite crystals [36]. Fig.3b shows the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of AgBr and the related complex photocatalysts. For CDs/Ag/AgBr, the characteristic peaks of CO and COC are observed at 1467, 1425, 1333, and 1257cm−1, revealing the existence of CDs in the complex photocatalyst [25]. The Raman and FT-IR spectra results provide a support to confirm the existence of carbon in the complex structure of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites.

Fig. 4 presents XPS analysis for investigating elemental composition and chemical states of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites. The full survey spectrum from Fig.4a demonstrates that the main elements in the sample are C, Ag, Br and In. Particular attention is paid to the element of In, which is from indium substrate. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, Ag 3d and Br 3d from CDs/Ag/AgBr are given in the Fig.4b–d. Fig.4b shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s, which can be fitted with three peaks located at 284.6, 285.4 and 288.2eV, corresponding to CC, CO and CO, respectively [37]. From the Fig.4c, two typical bands are observed at approximately 367.3eV and 373.2eV from Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 binding energies, respectively. These two bands could be further divided into four different peaks, which are 368.2, 367.3eV and 374.1, 373.2eV. And the peaks at 373.2 and 367.3eV can be attributed to the Ag+ in AgBr, and the peaks at 374.1 and 368.2eV belong to the Ag0 species [38]. For the Br elements, the peaks in Fig.4d located at 67.8eV and 68.9eV mainly correspond to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 in the form of Br− [39].

The UV–vis–NIR diffuse reflectance spectra of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr were compared in Fig. 5 . It is worth mentioning the colour of AgBr changed from light green 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 5, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 to brown with Ag-NPs formed on the surface of AgBr (Ag/AgBr), then the colour of Ag/AgBr from brown turns to dark brown after introduction of CDs (see insert picture Fig.5a–c). It can be seen that pure AgBr can absorb solar energy in the region below ca. 520nm, which is consistent with the previous report [40]. For Ag/AgBr, it shows strong absorption in a wide range of Vis and UV light, assigned to SPR absorption of plasmonic photocatalyst [41,42]. Significantly, CDs/Ag/AgBr exhibits a broad and intense absorption in the range of 520–1500nm, indicating that the intrinsic property of CDs gives rise to the enhancement of infrared-light photoresponse and plays a crucial factor in harvesting sunlight [43,44]. As a consequence, the complex ternary nanocomposites may possess extraordinary photocatalytic activity for degradation of TC.

In general, as major influencing factor, the loading amount of CDs in complex system should also be noted. The decomposition activities of TC with as-prepared photocatalyst were evaluated under Vis and NIR light irradiation. The orange trial reveals that TC molecule is resistant to photolysis under Vis and NIR light irradiation in the absence of a photocatalyst. As shown in Fig.6 a, after the addition of AgBr, Ag/AgBr or CDs/Ag/AgBr, TC degradation occurred under Vis light irradiation (λ
> 420nm). The degradation efficiencies of TC solution over AgBr and Ag/AgBr were about 78% and 82% in 60min. When assembling CDs on the surface of Ag/AgBr nanoparticles, the photocatalytic activity of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites was dramatically improved. The TC solution was completely degraded within 10min over CDs/Ag/AgBr photocatalyst during same experimental conditions.

The photocatalytic performance of different samples was further investigated under NIR light irradiation (λ
> 750nm) at room temperature, and the results are shown in Fig.6b. Specifically, the corresponding decomposition rate increases to nearly 100% after 30min over CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites under near-infrared light irradiation, whereas in the case of AgBr and Ag/AgBr, the degradation rate of the TC is about 47% and 51% for 60min. In addition, it was noted that strong absorption of electromagnetic radiation of water itself in the infrared region may lead it can convert the absorbed light energy into heat [45,46]. So, the control experiments of degrading TC over CDs/Ag/AgBr sample were carried out in the NIR light irradiation and heat at 70°C in the dark for 30min. The obtained results (Fig.6c) showed that the degradation rate of TC was 9%, confirming that the NIR catalytic activity of CDs/Ag/AgBr was mainly resulted from the photocatalysis reaction, not NIR light caused temperature effect. The kinetic behaviors of as-prepared photocatalysts for photodegradation of TC under NIR light were investigated according to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model [47]. It was found that the photodegradation process complies with first-order kinetics (Fig. S5a). We could see clearly that the rate constants of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites are up to 12.2 and 11.5 times faster than those of pure AgBr and Ag/AgBr under near-infrared light (Fig. S5b), respectively. In addition, the photocatalytic stability of CDs/Ag/AgBr was investigated under NIR light by recycling the composite in the repeated TC degradation experiments. The ten-time repeatability experiments of photodegradation of TC (10mg/L) by 100mg catalyst are shown in Fig.6d. After each degradation experiment, the catalyst was recycled by simple filtration, washed with water, and dried at 110°C for the next run. Although the degradation efficiency slightly decreased after each run, the catalyst still exhibited stable enough during the repeated experiments, indicating that CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites possessed extraordinary photocatalytic ability for organic compounds degradation with good stability under NIR light. These results clearly demonstrated that NIR photocatalytic performances of CDs/Ag/AgBr heterostructures could be attributed to the introduction of CDs into AgBr photocatalyst systems.

The photocurrent-time (PT) measurement was used to obtain transient photocurrent responses for the samples of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr under several on-off cycles of NIR light irradiation in Fig.7 a. Among all samples, CDs/Ag/AgBr has the highest photocurrent, while AgBr is the lowest one. In general, the higher photocurrent is, the higher e-h+ separation efficiency is [48]. Thus, the result of PT measurement suggests that CDs/Ag/AgBr composite holds stronger ability in separation of electron-hole pairs than AgBr, which demonstrates the positive role of CDs in enhancing the PT property of AgBr photoelectrodes. The EIS measurement was also performed to further elucidate the role of the CDs in the photogenerated charge separation and transport properties (Fig.7b). The arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plots shows similar trend as the PT properties. It can be clearly observed the arc radius on EIS Nynquist plot of the samples is CDs/Ag/AgBr<Ag/AgBr<AgBr, respectively. Since a smaller radius of the arc in the EIS indicates a smaller electron transfer resistance at the surface of photoelectrodes, a more effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and faster interfacial charge transfer are expected in the CDs/Ag/AgBr sample.

To further understand the higher photocatalytic activity of CDs/Ag/AgBr for the degradation of TC than the other two, we probe into a possible reason on the basis of experimental analysis. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of AgBr, Ag/AgBr and CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites were recorded in Fig.8 a for further understanding the electron transfer process in the nanocomposites. After combination with CDs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites obviously decreased. The fluorescence intensity is directly proportional to the electron-hole recombination probability, and lower PL intensity indicates the decrease in the recombination rate [49]. The results indicate that CDs/Ag/AgBr could have rapid implementation of the electron transfer, which is beneficial to the enhancement of electrons and holes separation and improves the photocatalytic performances. To further ensure the active species over CDs/Ag/AgBr for the photodegradation of TC under NIR light, different scavenges of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA), benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were introduced to quench h +,
O2 − and
OH in the photocatalytic process of TC degradation, respectively. From Fig.8b, it is can be seen that the removal yields of TC are also decreased to 19% and 52% in the presence of BQ and IPA, which indicates
O2 − and
OH play important roles in the oxidation of TC. Moreover, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TC over CDs/Ag/AgBr was not greatly depressed when the photocatalytic system is added EDTA, showing h + is not the main reactive species. In summary, the main reactive species involved are
O2 − and
OH, and the secondary reactive specie is h + in the degradation of TC. Furthermore, the ESR technique was further used to detect the presence of
OH and
O2 − radicals in the CDs/Ag/AgBr photocatalytic reaction system under NIR light irradiation. From Fig.8c and d, the four characteristic peaks of the DMPO-
OH adducts and six characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − adducts are observed, indicating that
OH and
O2 − radicals produced in CDs/Ag/AgBr reaction systems. However, without light irradiation, there are no the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH adducts or the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − adducts are observed. Therefore, according to the above results of ESR analysis, it can be again demonstrated that the
O2 − and
OH play dominant roles in the photocatalytic reactions.

Based on all of above experimental results, we speculate that the possible enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of CDs/Ag/AgBr photocatalyst in the photocatalytic process under NIR light irradiation (Fig. 9 ). It can be determined that that the Ag-NPs and CDs played an instrumental roles in making CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposite possess outstanding NIR light photocatalytic activity in virtue of the SPR effect induced by Ag-NPs with the collective oscillations of surface electrons and photo-generated electrons transfer property of CDs. The SPR effect of Ag-NPs locates at the NIR light region, which leads to the strong absorption to the NIR light, especially after introducing CDs in the complex system. In addition, the excellent conductivity of CDs can enhance the electron migration in order to promote the interfacial charge transfer and effectively hinder the recombination of electron/hole pairs. The reaction mechanism for degradation of TC over CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites under near-infrared light is discussed in the following steps. Firstly, as for the CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites, in view of the synergistic interaction of CDs and Ag-NPs, the NIR light could be absorbed availably by Ag-NPs. Meanwhile, as a result of the SPR effect induced local electromagnetic field, of a large amount of electron-hole pairs can be generated on Ag-NPs, that is to say, the electrons will be excited to the surface plasmon (SP) states, leaving positive charge (h+) below the Fermi level. Secondly, since the AgBr semiconductor has a higher work function than Ag (ɸ Ag =4.25eV, ɸ AgBr =5.3eV) [50], and the Fermi level of AgBr is lower than that of Ag. Thus, the well-defined interfaces facilitate the interfacial electron transfer from surface plasmon (SP) state of Ag to the conduction band (CB) of AgBr. Moreover, CDs have a lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level than AgBr, so electrons will be further transfered to CDs, promoting charge carrier separation and thus enhance the photocatalytic ability for target reaction. Finally, these electrons are trapped by O2 at the surface of photocatalyst or in the solution to form
O2 − radicals or other reactive oxygen species (
OH) to degrade TC molecules. Additionally, the h+ generated below the Fermi level of Ag-NPs can directly oxidize organic pollutants to small molecules. In short, due to the synergistic effect of CDs and Ag-NPs, the CDs/Ag/AgBr complex photocatalyst exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity among all the photocatalytic systems under near-infrared light irradiation.

The CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites were successfully fabricated through a facile precipitation-photoreduction method for the first time. The as-prepared CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposites as photocatalyst exhibits high-efficiency near-infrared light photocatalytic activity toward TC degradation. The enhanced photocatalytic activity for the CDs/Ag/AgBr nanocomposite could be ascribed to the extended absorption in the full spectrum, effective charge separation, and synergistic enhancement induced by surface plasmon resonance of Ag-NPs and the photogenerated change transfer properties of CDs in the complex nanocomposites. This work provides some insight into the design of carbon-based plasmonic photocatalysts for enhancing near-infrared light-driven photocatalytic activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (2012CB825803 and 2013CB932702), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11174092, 11474111, 51422207, 51132006, 51572179, 21471106, and 21501126), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123201110018), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20140310, BK20141305), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2014M560445, 2015T80581), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2016.10.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible light photocatalysis as a greener approach to photochemical synthesis
Nanomaterials for renewable energy production and storage
In situ growth of a ZnO nanowire network within a TiO2 nanoparticle film for enhanced dye-sensitized solar cell performance
Photocatalytic activity enhanced by plasmonic resonant energy transfer from metal to semiconductor
Separation and recovery of ZnS from sulfidized neutralization sludge via the hydration conversion of CaSO4 into bulk CaSO4·2H2O crystals
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
Novel BiVO4/InVO4 heterojunctions: Facile synthesis and efficient visible-light photocatalytic performance for the degradation of rhodamine B
